questions.q: Which transformation matrix operation combines translation, rotation, and scale into a single matrix?
questions.explanation: Matrix transformations are composed by multiplying matrices in the correct order. This allows combining translation, rotation, and scale into a single transformation that can be applied to vertices efficiently.

questions.q: In the model-view-projection (MVP) pipeline, what does the model matrix do?
options: Converts world coordinates to camera space
Transforms object coordinates to world space
questions.explanation: The model matrix transforms vertices from local object space into world space. The view matrix then converts to camera space, and projection applies perspective.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of using homogeneous coordinates in computer graphics?
options: Unified representation of translations, rotations, and projections using matrices
questions.explanation: Homogeneous coordinates allow all affine transformations (including translations) to be represented as matrix multiplications. Without them, translations would require vector addition instead of matrix multiplication.

questions.q: What does the perspective division by w achieve in the rendering pipeline?
options: Converts homogeneous to Cartesian coordinates and applies perspective foreshortening
questions.explanation: Dividing by the w-component converts homogeneous coordinates back to 3D Cartesian coordinates and applies perspective foreshortening. Objects farther away appear smaller because their w-values are larger.

questions.q: A 3D rotation can be represented as a 3x3 matrix or using a quaternion. What is a key advantage of quaternions?
options: They avoid gimbal lock and interpolate smoothly between rotations
They are faster to apply to vertices
They work in both 2D and 3D space
questions.explanation: Quaternions naturally avoid gimbal lock and provide smooth spherical interpolation (SLERP) between rotations. Matrices can suffer gimbal lock and linear interpolation produces unnatural rotation paths.

questions.q: In the transformation pipeline, the normal vector for lighting calculations should be transformed using which matrix?
options: Inverse transpose of the model matrix
questions.explanation: Normal vectors must be transformed by the inverse transpose of the model matrix to remain perpendicular to surfaces after non-uniform scaling. Using the model matrix directly corrupts normals for scaled geometry.

questions.q: What is the order of matrix multiplication for the complete MVP transformation?
questions.explanation: The standard order is Model (object to world) × View (world to camera) × Projection (camera to clip space). This transforms vertices from local space to world to camera to clip space sequentially.

questions.q: A scale transformation of (2, 1, 0.5) applied to a unit cube produces what result?
options: A rectangular box stretched along x-axis and compressed along z-axis
A unit cube with coordinates inverted
questions.explanation: Non-uniform scaling stretches different axes by different amounts. The x-axis scales by 2, y stays the same, and z compresses to half. This creates a rectangular box, not a cube.

questions.q: What does the view matrix encode in 3D graphics?
options: The camera position and orientation in world space
questions.explanation: The view matrix encodes the camera's position and orientation, transforming world coordinates into camera space where the camera is at the origin looking along the positive z-axis.

questions.q: In column-major matrix notation, how should a transformation be applied: M × v or v × M?
questions.explanation: In column-major convention (standard in graphics), vertices are column vectors and transformations apply as M × v. Row-major convention uses v × M, but produces identical results when matrices are transposed appropriately.
